summary: Joel Spolsky outlines his busy March 2006 conference schedule, which takes him across California and Texas for multiple speaking engagements. He'll be presenting on UI design and usability testing at ETech, participating in a startup panel at SXSW, screening the Aardvark'd documentary at SDWest, and appearing at EclipseCon. The post is a brief, informal travel update peppered with his characteristic dry humor and offhand jabs at conference culture and Java.
key_points: Joel observes that conferences disproportionately cluster in March, joking about a 'conference organizing school' that mandates the timing
He takes a shot at the value of startup conferences, suggesting founders should work on products instead of attending 'interactive festivals'
He parenthetically questions Java's relevance with the aside 'Is Java the new Cobol?'
money_quotes: Somewhere in the world, there's a conference organizing school, and they must teach you there that all conferences have to be held during March.
Here's a good startup decision: work on your products instead of hanging out at "interactive festivals" in Texas.
key_insight: Even while promoting his own conference appearances, Joel can't resist pointing out the irony that founders would be better off building products than attending startup panels.
